speaker: interviewer
text: --two of the interview with Sulia Rubin. We are in Saddle River, New Jersey. Sulia, you were telling me about your experience with the Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I developed-- I knew at that time that there is no place for us, that we must have our country back. And anyway, I knew from childhood on, whenever people used to say goodbye, they used to say, next year in Jerusalem. And I suddenly realized that I belonged someplace. And finally, with belonging someplace, I wound up in the United States. And I feel quite well here. But when I came for a visit to Israel, I had a feeling in certain places that I was there before-- that somehow, there is some kind of a connection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister share your beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, later on, yes. We became very close. And she was-- my parents were executed May the 7th, 1943. She died coming out of the tunnel. There were left about 250-280 Jews in a work camp in Novogrudok, and they build a tunnel under the ground. All of them came, except 80 or 100. And she was one of them who didn't. And I found out about her death quite incidentally. I don't know where she is buried. But I know it's in a wooded area not far from Novogrudok, called [NON-ENGLISH]. I know that she was caught by the local police-- she and another friend. I know she was raped. I know that they put a big stick through her genitals. I know that they tortured her before they shot her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how do I know it? When I was in the underground, and I went to another partisan brigade, there was a boy. I forgot even his name. Maybe I blocked it out somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: He laid there bound up in [NON-ENGLISH] in a mental institution. He hid on the tree. He was in that group, and he saw it all. And he told me how she perished. My parents were the last professionals in all of the-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- whole state-- in all of the county. And my sister watched them die through the window. The-- the-- the graves were dug very shallow, and they were covered with lime. And there were many people that were alive. And the grave shook for three days. I know that papa took morphine, and he went with a book under his arm. I don't know what he was reading there. My friends told me-- who came later on. My mother wouldn't take anything. She died with her eyes open. She told my friend, who is in Brooklyn, the only one who will survive will be Sulia. Because Rita will perish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sulia, I'd like to go back a little bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to before 1941, when you're in Novogrudok. And how did things begin to change? What were the first signs?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Soviets came. Hitler and Stalin, they found each other, sat down at the table, and ripped Poland apart. Lucky for us-- I cannot take away from the Russians that they saved us. Because if I would wait for the Americans, forget it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they ripped us apart. And our part belonged to Russia. And when the Soviets came in, they liquidated everything. They took away our house. The first time I found out that people live by meter-- I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] --they give you with so many, so many meters to live on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a large house before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So they left us in two rooms. They told my parents that they can practice. But they put on such big taxes on them that they were forced to join the government lab, together with the other dentists and doctors. And they supposedly paid my mother for her chairs, for all the equipment-- pennies. Funny-- when I visited in 1969, I saw the same chairs with my mother-- my mother's monogram in the same lab. They still have it. They haven't improved. No wonder they, all of them, haven't got any teeth. [LAUGHTER] And life has become so that the parents were afraid of the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were afraid to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they afraid of you, your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother told me only one thing. She, the old fighter, who sat six months for communism-- [LAUGHS] --in jail, said, if I ever join a communist organization, she would commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had been a communist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right! She fought against the Tsar. That was the only way-- against the pogroms, against the Cossacks. All the intelligentsia belonged. Now that they were communists, they were being killed left and right by the whoever! They thought that communism would be the savior-- equality. The book is beautiful. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: People don't live up to it. And-- and I used to say, mother, how could the book be beautiful if Marx-- he was Jewish, and he converted?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, the man is not stable.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: If you didn't belong here, and they didn't belong there, so what is he? She said, you have a point.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] But anyway, when they came in, she said, oh, my God. Look what happened-- barefoot and hungry they ran from Warsaw home, and barefoot and hungry they came back. Suddenly, everything disappeared from store-- from the stores. There were lines for sugar, lines for everything. And I found out that as much as I dislike the Nazis, the Germans-- when they came for a day, they fix everything--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --up like they would stay for a whole year. The Russians come to live for a lifetime, they fix everything up for a day. So it breaks down-- nothing works. But they are happy people. They take out a-- a-- a accordion in the middle of the street. They play. They sing. And they dance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're likeable. And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they'll share bread with you. They're likable. But the government was something else. I got in trouble with-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --can you stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and so you're telling me about your life under communism, and your mother was talking to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a culture shock, because we were thought that you have to read a book, form your own opinion-- no, no, no, no! You have to read their version of the book. It's louder. Their version of the book--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. I gotta stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Our interview was interrupted because of some sound problems, and now we've moved to another room where it's more quiet. And Sulia, you were just telling me about your life under the Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, we were thought-- taught that we have to read and make up our mind. Lo and behold I came to an education-- I wasn't allowed to say what-- what I thought. I had to say by the book-- which you read. They had written up. You didn't have to read the book. I don't know what you call it in English. We call it [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is like they write you down what to say-- what the book is all about-- and this is what you say. There is a Russian writer by the name of Chernyshevsky, and he wrote years ago a book, And in this book, there is a woman who fought, like my mother, for her identity. And she built the first communal factory, where everybody had a share in it. She gave women a job-- a sewing job-- and everybody had a communal interest in the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said that it was an example of communism. And I said, in those times, there was no communism. To me, Chernyshevsky was a utopist. I means, he dreamt about something, but it did not exist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I was the only girl in school, my first language was Russian. My second one was Polish. Jewish, I learned from my husband. I speak six. The others that I understand, I don't. But the languages I write, and read, and think in are six. And I can translate momentarily from one to the other. Well, I wrote it down what I thought-- that he was-- he wasn't a communist. There was no communism there. Nobody thought of it to date. He was a utopist-- utopia. He dreamt about something. I was called to the principal, given a five-- an A in my Russian-- failed in political thoughts-- simply bourgeois. And they called my parents. I did not know what my sin was. I came home. My father said, you think for yourself at home. In school, you say what's written-- [LAUGHS] --on the paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to mama, you see what happened? In 1937, I had affidavits to go to Palestine. And you said you will not go there, because your daughters will be made milkmaids in the kibbutz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: She the smart one-- my father knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he have the affidavits made?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a ardent Zionist, and there was a part of a library in there, in the Negev. That is the name of it. He knew everybody in the kibbutzim. He was active in it. He was an activist. And I don't know how he got the affidavits to go to Palestine. But my mother said her daughters will be two maids-- milkmaids-- [LAUGHS] --in a kibbutz. She was snobbish. She didn't want to. And life has changed-- changed to a point that our high school, which is called gymnasium, was approximately four classes of gymnasium, two classes of [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was like, I would say, high school here and two years of college. Because I used to help my daughters in chemistry, so I know that I was on a higher level. And I did not graduate, I want you to know. Because I was too young at that time. Funny to think that I was, some time, a girl too young. And misery started-- with that Jewish life was thwarted. Suddenly, now that we weren't allowed to go to the synagogue, I wanted to go there. Forbidden things are something that draw you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was drawn so to my Jewishness while it was a burden in Poland. And I wouldn't mind to get rid of it, because it was hard. Now I wanted it so badly that I tried even on a Friday night to put on candles. And my mother used to say, what are you doing? You want us arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- in Poland, we were not allowed to speak. Those who went to school were not allowed to speak Jewish in the street, because it supposedly made you forget Polish. Then you weren't fluent in it. Now we were forbidden not only to talk Jewish, we were forbidden to think Jewish. And we disliked the system something horribly. It wasn't a long time. In 1941, the Nazi's were so smart that instead of coming to our town first, they landed near Minsk first. And all the roads were cut off. So there was no place to run. Some people tried to get through on bikes, and whatever, but most of us were caught-- sitting ducks.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was sudden? There was no warning?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a blitzkrieg. What did they have to bomb in this historical town? OK. This town was approximately-- it-- it's a historical town, because the Polish Queen Jadwiga, married the Lithuanian King Jagiello, and Litwa-- Lithuania-- was-- the-- were pagans. And they accepted Christianity.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had the church. The Fara, it was called, where they were married. Before that, there are ruins of a Lithuanian King Gedimind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our Polish poet, Adam Michiewicz, was born in Novogrudok. His poetry-- you see, while my education was rounded, because I knew Tennyson. I knew all the writers here. I read Kipling. It was a must. You didn't know anything about ours if you wouldn't have here a movie, You wouldn't know that Henryk Sienkiewicz existed-- the writer. He wrote Mister Wolodyjowski, who had a wife, Bashka-- Basia-- Bess-- Bessie it would be in English-- who rode a horse, and wanted to be equal to men. And when I wanted to go to the underground, my mother said that God has punished her. I read and I wanted to be Bashka. Which was true. And when the Germans came, life was finished. They came the summer of 1941. The first ones they shot were 50 Jews on the marketplace, right across from our windows. First, they bombed us. And then they took our priest, [NON-ENGLISH]. Nobody will mention his name. He was a quiet hero. And my professor of history, Augustine-- there was a King Augustine who was a lover of Catherine the Great. He descended from those-- was a lovely man. And en masse, at a mass in church, in Fara, he said to the Poles, that be careful. The Jews go first, and we are next. This is only the occupation. And somebody of the-- your own-- just like they did now in Poland with Popieluszko before-- they gave him out. And they were hung-- the priest with my professor in the middle of the marketplace. And we were all chased out, and we had to watch it. It was a fearful time. While our Nazarethen nuns-- true-- they were raped by the Russians when they came in. And they were disbanded. And one of them, a hunchback, we hidden-- hid in our house. She was our friend later on. True, the Russians did horrible things, but they did it to everybody. They did it to the Jews, and non-Jews-- to everybody. Whereas the Germans, when they started-- I wouldn't say Germans-- the Nazis-- when they started-- first of all, were the Jews. I guess if there would blacks, they would be the first. But we had no black people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first were the Jews. Then, of course, came the Catholics. The Russians didn't take off the bell from the church. The Germans-- supposedly they needed it for ammunition. They took off the bells from every church.

speaker: interviewee
text: The synagogue-- I don't have to tell you what they did to the synagogue. They burned Jews in the synagogue alive. But it was a unbelievable nightmare, that one thought, I will go to sleep. I'll wake up. And it will be all gone. Because it's impossible to believe that in the 20th century, human beings could do something to you. Animals wouldn't do it. They don't do it to their own kind. They-- [LAUGHS] --do it to a different animal-- it just-- unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, December the 7th, 1941, we were told we are going to a ghetto. And we were chased out of the houses. How can you ever believe in any resistance, when you are a peaceful people who never even hunted? My uncle rode a horse. He was in the cavalry. [LAUGHS] But who ever heard of killing anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid that my dog will be caught by the-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --by the dogcatchers and will be dead. And when I saw a dead bird, I buried it. Because anything dead is sacred. You didn't even kill a worm, let alone you would kill people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was unbelievable. And people were-- although there were rumors. But my mother used to say, oh! I know the Germans from the first world war. They are cultural people. Don't listen to the stories. Don't forget. The radio was limited. There was no television. There was certainly no satellites. Nobody knew anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, when we were all gathered in the courthouse, and it was snow already-- and my mother had tiny, tiny feet. I don't know if they were size two or three. And she walked out with shoes with open toes. And all I thought about-- that she'll freeze the toes. And I remember, [NON-ENGLISH]. I rubbed her toes. And suddenly, my sister and I became the adults, and my parents were the children. And I was-- I was a child!

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born 1924-- 15 when the Russians came, 17 when the Germans. At 18 I was married! [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I say I went from diapers to bed-- no anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] Who-- I mean, it was a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we were segregated-- left, right, right, left. We went to the right. My cousin with her two children went to the left. We were-- who knew that the right was life and the left was death?

speaker: interviewee
text: And people were-- were collected on trucks and taken behind the-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- the armory, where the army stood. The town, the wood-- woods, were called [NON-ENGLISH]. And there were farmers-- were digging holes there-- trenches. Our people weren't taken to a-- to a concentration camp. They were shot right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I saw many shot. Where we were, in the room, we were put-- there were no windows. They were barred with wood. And you could see. And suddenly, papa said, oh my God. And he fainted. Mama caught papa. Rita was-- and I, like an idiot, went through the cracks to look. And I saw my-- my cousin Mina with the two children on one truck. Rowik was six years younger than I. So he-- he was like 11. Ruthi was three years younger than him. And my cousin was a gorgeous woman. And she won beauty contests. And I hear the Nazis saying to her, [NON-ENGLISH]. Come down. She says, [NON-ENGLISH]-- my children-- said, I can't. She says, I'm dying with them. It's a horrible thing to say. I lost respect to many men during the war. Mothers died with their children, not fathers-- not fathers. Not one father went holding the children to the grave-- unless he was sent there. Willingly, only mothers stayed with the children. And since then, I'm holding my two children. And I run every night to the grave. And then I see my uncle and my aunt on another truck, and the whole family-- my cousins-- all gone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They told us they were sending them to work. None of them ever came back. From there, we were taken to the ghetto. We got assigned to us a house for professionals only. There were doctors, dentists whatever. There was, right away-- the Germans are so clever, they organized the Jewish police, and the Jewish judenrat-- a Jewish government. Nobody mentions a man by the name of Czernowski. He was one of our lawyers. And when they asked him for a list of the women and children, he committed suicide. He didn't give them anything. I don't blame people who ruled us to save their lives-- fine. But if they were told to give you-- to give lists, they should have forewarned the people, knocked at every door, and said look. I'm giving them the list, because I have to. But you run, and try to save yourself, because you know what it is for. They didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after we were in the ghetto, I lived through two executions. One, I was left to live, because we were childrens-- children of professionals. The second execution was in the summer of '42, where I decided that I do not exist. I will not register with them. I will not have them a number. Because if I'll run someplace, they can not take revenge over my parents or my sister. So I used to take her number, but I didn't have any. I only had the yellow-- the yellow star. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked on the cemetery-- on the old German cemetery in Novogrudok. But actually, we dug graves, and planted the Impatiens, or whatever. I had a grave by a German who got killed-- [LAUGHS] --in 1917. His name was William Loch And every day, I hung my little jacket over there. We had very few clothing at that time. And I used to say a prayer. Thank you, William Loch, that you died in the first world war, otherwise you would be here now. So it's one less. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was my prayer. I didn't pray much. I was angry with God. I thought he was a sadist. I could forgive him everything but the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to say, how could you sit there and watch babies being killed? What did they ever do? And there was always that debate between me and the unknown.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And somehow I became-- it was a paradox-- that although I did not believe exactly in God, I started to believe that-- although I am a fatalist-- it will be, whatever it'll be-- but God helps those who help themselves-- and looking for a way out. While I was in the ghetto, I saw people smuggling in through the-- at night, after curfew. It wasn't finished yet. The barbed wire wasn't put down. The fence wasn't finished. It was very primitive yet. And I would talk to them and hide them in the attic. And I never told my parents. I never told my sister. Because if I would tell them, I think they would give it out first thing to the Jewish police. They would think that I am jeopardizing the life of the whole ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And while I worked on the cemetery, there was an old German. He must have been around 40. [LAUGHTER] It shows how you think when you are 17, that 40 is old. He told me he is a farmer and that he had two children. He would bring us bread-- not the others, but me. And I would share it-- give each one a slice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And walking once to work, I saw a sign-- a bandit by then-- a Jew-- by the name of Tuvia Bielski is given for his head any information-- so many, and so many. I don't remember how many-- how many-- the German money-- some-- some-- some-- some kind of a ridiculous sum it was. But I said, by golly, to this Jew I'm going to go-- but how? Who-- You saw a sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: A, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: A wanted sign.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the kiosk-- a wanted sign. And I saw Jew-- [NON-ENGLISH]. So I immediately looked. You were not allowed to walk with the sidewalk. You had to walk with a-- with the street. And you were not allowed to look at the German. You had to keep your head down But it shows you what bashert means-- what's meant to be. There was one killer who used to go out in the morning, and before breakfast, take his son. And he would shoot a Jew so he would have a better appetite together with the child. And one time, I see shining boots near me. And I picked up the head. Lo and behold it was this German-- forgot his name-- without the son.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started talking to me. And I am all dirty in the rubble, working after the bombing. And I answered in German. And he says, and where did you learn German? And I tell him, in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said he needs me. And I-- he found me a job. I was off the rubble. [INAUDIBLE]
